mapping.a_element: quasiclassical Eilenberger theory governing superconducting pair potentials and magnetic fields
mapping.b_element: Abelian Higgs model with two charged scalar fields governing vortex structure and electromagnetic fields

mapping.a_element: π-phase shift of the pair potential at FFLO nodal planes or vortex cores
mapping.b_element: nested pipelike structure of vortices with layered electromagnetic fields

mapping.a_element: local paramagnetic moments enhanced by sharp peak states in local density of states
mapping.b_element: longitudinal electric currents in opposite directions within charged pipes

mapping.a_element: vortex core as a region of modified superconducting order
mapping.b_element: geometric center of the vortex with concentrated magnetic field at a distance

brief_justification: While both papers study vortex structures in systems with broken symmetries, Paper A relies on quasiclassical superconductivity theory (Eilenberger equations) to describe FFLO modulations and paramagnetic effects, whereas Paper B uses a field-theoretic Abelian Higgs model to analyze pipelike electromagnetic currents in two-component condensates. The underlying mechanisms (quasiclassical vs. topological field theory) and governing equations differ fundamentally.
